analogy: Think of "Introducing Western Sociologists" through the chapter's own opening images: 65 INTRODUCING WESTERN SOCIOLOGISTS CHAPTER 4 INTRODUCING WESTERN SOCIOLOGISTS Sociology is sometimes called the child of the ‘age of revolution’. This is because it was born in 19th century Western Europe, after revolutionary changes in the preceding three centuries that decisively changed the way people... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Introducing Western Sociologists is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that 65 INTRODUCING WESTERN SOCIOLOGISTS CHAPTER 4 INTRODUCING WESTERN SOCIOLOGISTS Sociology is sometimes called the child of the ‘age of revolution’.

The text explains that this is because it was born in 19th century Western Europe, after revolutionary changes in the preceding three centuries that decisively changed the way people lived.

The text explains that three revolutions paved the way for the emergence of sociology: the Enlightenment, or the scientific revolution; the French Revolution; and the Industrial Revolution.

The text explains that these processes completely transformed not only European society, but also the rest of the world as it came into contact with Europe.

The text explains that in this chapter the key ideas of three sociological thinkers: Karl Marx, Emile Durkheim and Max Weber will be discussed.

The text explains that as part of the classical tradition of sociology, they laid the foundation of the subject.

The text explains that their ideas and insights have remained relevant even in the contemporary period.

The text explains that of course, these ideas have also been subjected to criticism and have undergone major modifications.

The text explains that but since ideas a b o u t s o c i e t y a re t h e m s e l v e s influenced by social conditions, we begin with a few words about the context in which sociology emerged.

The text explains that tHE CONTEXT OF SOCIOLOGY The modern era in Europe and the conditions of modernity that we take for granted today were brought about by three major processes.

The text explains that these were: the Enlightenment or dawning of the ‘age of reason’; the quest for political sovereignty embodied in the French Revolution; and the system of mass manufacture inaugurated by the Industrial Revolution.

The text explains that since these have been discussed at length in Chapter 1 of Introducing Sociology, here we will only mention some of the intellectual consequences of these momentous changes.

The text explains that what sorts of changes were these three processes associated with?

The text explains that 66 UNDERSTANDING SOCIETY The Enlightenment During the late 17th and 18th centuries, Western Europe saw the emergence of radically new ways of thinking about the world.

The text explains that refered to as ‘The Enlightenment’, these new philosophies established the human being at the centre of the universe, and rational thought as the central feature of the human being.

The text explains that the ability to think rationally and critically transformed the individual human being into both the producer and the user of all knowledge, the ‘knowing subject’.

The text explains that the first was the systematic application of science and technology to industrial production, particularly the invention of new machines and the harnessing of new sources of power.

The text explains that productive forces refer to all the means or factors of production such as land, labour, technology, sources of energy (such as electricity, coal, petroleum and so on).

The text explains that serfs and small peasants were thrown off their lands and deprived of their earlier sources of livelyhood.

The text explains that they then congregated in cities looking for ways to survive, and the pressure of the laws and police forced them to work in the newly built factories.

The text explains that these ties or social solidarities exerted pressure on individuals to conform to the norms and expectations of the group.

The text explains that even when the social phenomenon is small — like a family or a theatre group — we can directly see only the individuals who make up the collectivity; we cannot see the collectivity itself.

The text explains that although not directly observable, social facts were indirectly observable through patterns of behaviour.

The text explains that while the source of traditional authority was custom and precedence, charismatic authority derived from divine sources or the ‘gift of grace’, and rational-legal authority was based on legal demarcation of authority.

The text explains that social Fact: Aspects of social reality that are related to collective patterns of behaviour and beliefs, which are not created by individuals but exert pressure on them and influence their behaviour.

The text explains that what are the various components of a mode of production?

The text explains that why do classes come into conflict, according to Marx?

The text explains that what is the difference between ‘mechanical’ and ‘organic’ solidarity?

The text explains that show, with examples, how moral codes are indicators of social solidarity.

The text explains that what is special or different about the kind of objectivity needed in social science?

The text explains that can you identify any ideas or theories which have led to the formation of social movements in India in recent times?

The text explains that try to find out what Marx and Weber wrote about India.

The text explains that can you think of reasons why we should study the work of thinkers who died long ago?

The text explains that what could be some reasons to not study them?

The text explains that max Weber: An Intellectual Portrait, Anchor Books, New York.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society, Chapter 4: Introducing Western Sociologists

schemes.point: Production relations were based on community property (since individual private property did not yet exist) and included tribal forms of hunting or gathering which were the... (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch4 pg 5)

schemes.name: In The Communist Manifesto
schemes.point: In The Communist Manifesto (which was also a programme of action), Marx and Engels presented their views in a clear and concise manner. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch4 pg 6)

prelims_traps: Do not treat 65 INTRODUCING WESTERN SOCIOLOGISTS and CHAPTER 4 as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Introducing Western Sociologists; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Introducing Western Sociologists through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 65 INTRODUCING WESTERN SOCIOLOGISTS and CHAPTER 4 to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
